1: David comes to Nob (nōḇ), the priestly city, to Ahimelech the priest. Ahimelech comes out trembling (ḥāraḏ) to meet David — his fear signals that David's arrival without a military escort or royal commission is unusual and alarming. Nob is identified with the ridge northeast of Jerusalem, visible from the city (Isaiah 10:32 describes an Assyrian army shaking its fist at Nob, visible from the approach to Jerusalem). The city housed the displaced priestly community after Shiloh's destruction.
2: David gives a cover story: he is on a secret royal mission and has arranged to meet his men at a designated place. The fabrication is necessary for Ahimelech's protection — if Ahimelech knows David is a fugitive, any assistance he provides constitutes harboring. David's deception protects the priest by limiting his culpable knowledge. The tragic irony is that Doeg the Edomite is already present (v7), making the deception ultimately fatal for Ahimelech.
3: David asks for bread — 'five loaves or whatever is here.' The quantity (five loaves) reflects the scale of a small group's immediate need. The 'whatever is here' suggests he is not demanding but requesting whatever is available. In Hebrew hospitality culture, providing food for travelers was a social obligation; Ahimelech's concern is not about generosity but about the sacred status of what he has available.
4: Ahimelech says he has no ordinary (ḥōl) bread — only the bread of the Presence (leḥem happānîm), the showbread that sits before YHWH on the table in the sanctuary. The showbread consisted of twelve loaves arranged in two rows of six on a gold table in the Holy Place, renewed each Sabbath. Leviticus 24:5-9 specifies that the old loaves were reserved for Aaron and his sons to eat in the sanctuary. Giving this bread to David would be a deviation from its prescribed sacred use — but Ahimelech is willing to consider it if the men are ritually qualified.
5: Ahimelech's condition: the men must have 'kept themselves from women' (šāmrû minnāšîm) — the ritual sexual abstinence required of warriors in holy war (cf. Deuteronomy 23:9-14; 2 Samuel 11:11 where Uriah refuses to sleep with his wife because of the sacred war context). David assures him the men have been ritually pure for three days. The priest's question treats David's reported mission as a sacred-war context in which the showbread's use could be authorized by emergency necessity.
6: Ahimelech gives David the bread of the Presence that was removed from before YHWH to be replaced by fresh bread that day — it was the Sabbath and fresh loaves had just been set out. The timing detail — the old loaves being removed exactly when David arrived — is either providential coincidence or narrative tidiness. The showbread had completed its week before YHWH; the old loaves were being returned to priestly use; David receives them in the moment of transition.
7: On that day a servant of Saul was detained before YHWH (neʿĕṣār lipnê YHWH) — Doeg the Edomite, the chief of Saul's shepherds. The phrase 'detained before YHWH' likely refers to a period of ritual fulfillment at the sanctuary — perhaps fulfilling a vow or completing a period of purification that required remaining at the sanctuary. Doeg's presence is incidental, not investigative, but it makes him a witness to everything that follows.
8: David asks if there is a spear or sword available — he came in such haste he brought no weapon. The absence of a personal weapon underscores the emergency nature of his departure from Gibeah: David left quickly with nothing, making the trip to Nob necessary for basic equipment. A court warrior who appears at a sanctuary without a weapon is conspicuously under-equipped.
9: Ahimelech tells David that the sword of Goliath the Philistine — the one David struck down in the Valley of Elah — is here, wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod. If David wants it, he may take it; there is no other. The storage of Goliath's sword as a trophy at the sanctuary is consistent with ANE practice of depositing significant war spoils at temples — the sword that YHWH gave David victory with belongs in YHWH's sanctuary. David accepts it: 'There is none like it; give it to me.'
10: David rises and flees from Saul and comes to Achish the king of Gath. Achish of Gath appears in later narratives as a Philistine ruler who eventually becomes David's patron (1 Samuel 27-29). The Amarna letters and various ANE sources identify Gath (Tell es-Safi/Tel Zafit) as a significant Philistine city-state; its king had diplomatic and administrative relations with surrounding polities. David's flight to Gath — the home city of Goliath — is a desperate gamble: he is seeking refuge with the enemy whose champion he killed.
11: The servants of Achish recognize David: 'Is this not David the king of the land? Did they not sing of this one in the dances, Saul has struck his thousands, and David his ten thousands?' The victory song of 18:7 has traveled to Philistia — David's military reputation is known across the region. Being identified in an enemy court as 'king of the land' immediately threatens David's safety; the servants are alerting Achish to the political significance of the refugee.
12: David takes these words to heart and is very afraid of Achish the king of Gath. His fear is rational — he has been identified as Israel's de facto king by Achish's own servants. A foreign king who harbors a rival king's designated successor may be seen as a provocateur; worse, Achish might return David to Saul for political conciliation. David must immediately change how he is perceived.
13: David alters his behavior before them — acting as if he were insane, making marks on the doors of the gate and letting saliva run down his beard. Feigned madness as a self-protective stratagem was known in the ancient world: Odysseus feigned madness to avoid conscription for the Trojan War (Apollodorus, Epitome 3.7). In the ANE, madness was considered a divine affliction; harming a madman was considered an offense against the deity who had struck him. David is exploiting this religio-cultural protection by simulating divine judgment upon himself.
14: Achish says to his servants: 'Look, you see the man is mad. Why then have you brought him to me? Do I lack madmen, that you have brought this fellow to behave as a madman in my presence? Shall this fellow come into my house?' Achish's irritated dismissal achieves precisely what David needed: he is seen as harmlessly insane rather than as a political threat. The king's rhetorical question — 'do I lack madmen?' — suggests that the presence of madmen at court was not unusual (possibly as diviners or figures associated with divine judgment), but that importing a foreign one was pointless.
15: David departs from Gath to the cave of Adullam (1 Samuel 22:1). The Gath episode ends with David's successful deception — he has extracted himself from a dangerous situation through wit rather than force. The Psalm headings of Pss 34 and 56 attribute them to this period; Ps 34 opens 'I will bless YHWH at all times; his praise shall continually be in my mouth' — the voice of a man who has just escaped what seemed like certain death through unexpected divine provision.
